content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

message.content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the prompt file to understand the exercise.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event.
50	
51	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
52	  the damage amount.
53	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
54	
55	## Examples
56	
57	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
58	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
59	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
60	
61	### Building block of 3 alike components
62	
63	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
64	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
65	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
66	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
67	
68	### "Alike" components
69	
70	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
71	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
72	
73	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
74	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
75	
76	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
77	
78	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
79	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
80	
81	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
82	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
83	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
84	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
85	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
86	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
87	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
88	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
89	  added at the very end
90	
91	### Modifier thresholds
92	
93	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
94	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
95	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
96	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
97	  surcharge applies only if cursed
98	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
99	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
100	
101	### Deductible per damage event
102	
103	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
104	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
105	  per damaged item)
106	
107	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
108	
109	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
110	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
111	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
112	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
113	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
114	
115	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
116	
117	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
118	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
119	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
120	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
121	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
122	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
123	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
124	  deductible: 500 − 100)
125	
126	### Multiple items of the same type
127	
128	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
129	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
130	> two?
131	
132	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000)
133	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
134	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
135	  separate damage with its own deductible
136	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
137	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
138	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
139	  whole claim is rejected
140	
141	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
142	
143	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
144	  (rounded up)
145	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
146	  (rounded down)
147	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
148	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
149	
150	### Edge cases
151	
152	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
153	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
154	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
155	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
156	  to stdout
157	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
158	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
159	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
160	  and writes an error description to stderr
161	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
162	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
163	  stderr
164	
165	## Integration examples
166	
167	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
168	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
169	
170	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
171	
172	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
173	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
174	- premium: 165 G
175	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
176	
177	### Long-standing customer's second contract
178	
179	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
180	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
181	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
182	> insurance surcharge still apply?
183	
184	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
185	  in the scenario
186	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
187	- premium: 160 G
188	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
189	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
190	  = 160 G)
191	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
192	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
193	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
194	  history.
195	
196	## CLI input/output format
197	
198	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
199	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
200	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
201	stdout.
202	
203	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
204	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
205	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
206	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
207	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
208	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
209	
210	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
211	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
212	
213	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
214	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G).
215	
216	### JSON Schema (normative)
217	
218	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
219	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
220	
221	**Input (stdin):**
222	
223	```json
224	{
225	  "type": "object",
226	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
227	  "properties": {
228	    "customer": {
229	      "type": "object",
230	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
231	      "properties": {
232	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
233	      }
234	    },
235	    "steps": {
236	      "type": "array",
237	      "items": {
238	        "oneOf": [
239	          {
240	            "description": "Quote step",
241	            "required": ["op", "items"],
242	            "properties": {
243	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
244	              "items": {
245	                "type": "array",
246	                "items": {
247	                  "type": "object",
248	                  "required": ["type"],
249	                  "properties": {
250	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
251	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
252	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
253	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
254	                  }
255	                }
256	              }
257	            }
258	          },
259	          {
260	            "description": "Claim step",
261	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
264	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
265	              "incident": {
266	                "type": "object",
267	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
268	                "properties": {
269	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
270	                  "damages": {
271	                    "type": "array",
272	                    "items": {
273	                      "type": "object",
274	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
275	                      "properties": {
276	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
277	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
278	                      }
279	                    }
280	                  }
281	                }
282	              }
283	            }
284	          }
285	        ]
286	      }
287	    }
288	  }
289	}
290	```
291	
292	**Output (stdout):**
293	
294	```json
295	{
296	  "type": "object",
297	  "required": ["results"],
298	  "properties": {
299	    "results": {
300	      "type": "array",
301	      "items": {
302	        "oneOf": [
303	          {
304	            "description": "Quote result",
305	            "required": ["premium"],
306	            "properties": {
307	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
308	            }
309	          },
310	          {
311	            "description": "Claim result",
312	            "required": ["payout"],
313	            "properties": {
314	              "payout": { "type": "integer" }
315	            }
316	          }
317	        ]
318	      }
319	    }
320	  }
321	}
322	```
323	
324	### Schema example
325	
326	Stdin:
327	
328	```json
329	{
330	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
331	  "steps": [
332	    {
333	      "op": "quote",
334	      "items": [
335	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
336	      ]
337	    },
338	    {
339	      "op": "claim",
340	      "policy": 0,
341	      "incident": {
342	        "cause": "fire",
343	        "damages": [
344	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
345	        ]
346	      }
347	    }
348	  ]
349	}
350	```
351	
352	Stdout shape:
353	
354	```json
355	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>}]}
356	```
357	
358	## Expected Output Files
359	
360	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
361	- Test files
362	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
363	
364	## Constraints
365	
366	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
367	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
368	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
369	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
370

# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000)
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G).

### JSON Schema (normative)

The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.

**Input (stdin):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
  "properties": {
    "customer": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
      "properties": {
        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote step",
            "required": ["op", "items"],
            "properties": {
              "op": { "const": "quote" },
              "items": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["type"],
                  "properties": {
                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim step",
            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
            "properties": {
              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
              "incident": {
                "type": "object",
                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
                "properties": {
                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
                  "damages": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {
                      "type": "object",
                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
                      "properties": {
                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Output (stdout):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["results"],
  "properties": {
    "results": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote result",
            "required": ["premium"],
            "properties": {
              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim result",
            "required": ["payout"],
            "properties": {
              "payout": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

input.prompt:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office - insurance policy management CLI for magical items
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: from /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/prompt.md

Full requirements from the spec:

The system supports two operations through a CLI (src/cli.ts) reading JSON from stdin and writing JSON to stdout:
- quote: computes premium for a list of items
- claim: processes a damage report against an existing policy (referenced by zero-based step index)

Item values and base premiums:
- Sword: 1000 G value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
- Components (runes, moonstones): 250 G value, 25 G base premium each; 3 alike components form a "block" at 60 G

Premium modifiers:
- Cursed items: +50% risk surcharge (item-specific, applied to that item's base premium)
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment >= 5): +30% risk surcharge (item-specific)
- Long-standing customers (yearsWithMHPCO >= 2): -20% loyalty discount (policy-wide)
- First insurance: +10% initial assessment surcharge (each item in quote is treated as first insurance)
- Follow-up contract (each contract after the first): -15% discount (policy-wide)
- 5 G processing fee added at the very end
- Final premium rounded UP to whole G (in MHPCO's favor)

Block rules: 3 alike (same type) components get a block at 60 G; 4 runes = 100 G (no block, block requires exactly 3); 7 runes = 175 G (block of 3 + 4 singles = 60+100); 3 runes + 3 moonstones = 120 G (two separate blocks).

Claim processing:
- 100 G deductible per damage event (per damage entry)
- Items with enchantment >= 8: reimbursed at 50% of damage amount
- Items made of dragon material: fully reimbursed
- If both apply: 50% rule wins (then deductible)
- Final payout rounded DOWN to whole G

Error cases (CLI exits non-zero, writes to stderr):
- Unknown item type in quote
- Claim references item not in policy
- Claim damage amount negative
- More damage entries of a type than policy actually covers

Integration examples:
- Newcomer with cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3), 0 years, first quote: 165 G (100 base + 50 curse + 10 first = 160 + 5 fee)
- Long-standing customer (3 years), second quote, cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7): 160 G (100 + 50 curse + 30 high-ench - 20 loyalty + 10 first - 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee)
- Empty item list: 5 G (just fee)

Modifier scope clarification: item-specific (cursed, high-enchantment) apply to that item's base premium. Policy-wide (loyalty, first insurance per item, follow-up contract) - though spec says first insurance applies per item.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints. Create the test list with it.todo() entries in src/claim-office.spec.ts. Focus on base functionality from the spec. Order from simple to complex.

3. **Quote - component blocks** (6 tests): The block-of-3 rule and variations from the spec
4. **Quote - item-specific modifiers** (5 tests): Curse, high-enchantment, scoping
5. **Quote - policy-wide modifiers** (6 tests): Loyalty, first insurance, follow-up
6. **Quote - rounding and fee** (2 tests)
7. **Quote - integration examples** (2 tests): The two worked examples from the spec
8. **Quote - error cases** (1 test): Unknown item type
9. **Claim - base reimbursement** (3 tests): Standard payout + deductible per damage
10. **Claim - special clauses** (4 tests): Enchantment >= 8, dragon material, both
11. **Claim - rounding** (1 test)
12. **Claim - error cases** (3 tests): Item not in policy, negative amount, too many damage entries

**Design notes**:
- Tests use the `quote` and `claim` API directly (not the CLI), since the CLI layer is a thin JSON adapter. The CLI itself can be developed/tested separately later.
- Each test corresponds to a concrete behaviour from the spec or an example, with expected G values pre-computed.
- All thresholds explicitly tested (enchantment exactly 5, exactly 8, customer exactly 2 years).
- Advanced/excluded for later: CLI stdin/stdout integration tests, complex multi-step scenarios beyond the two spec examples, exhaustive block combinations, enchantment 0 edge cases.

content.text:
All 4 tests pass. Now I'll evaluate refactoring opportunities.

## Refactor Phase Evaluation

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

- **`quote`**: Clearly describes producing an insurance quote. Fits.
- **`basePremiumFor`**: Clearly describes returning a base premium for an item. Fits.
- **`BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`**: Self-describing lookup table. Fits.
- **`PROCESSING_FEE`**: Clear domain term. Fits.
- **`itemsTotal`**: Describes accumulated items premium. Fits.

Decision: Keep all current names — they reveal intent well.

### Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

```typescript
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;                                              // const:1 + binding:1 = 2
const BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number> = {                 // binding:1 + 3 keys(const:1)+3 values(const:1) = 7
  sword: 100, amulet: 60, staff: 80,
};

function basePremiumFor(item: { type: string }): number {              // binding:1 (item)
  return BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE[item.type];                              // bindings:2 (BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE, item.type) = 2
}

export function quote(_customer, items): number {                      // bindings:2 (_customer, items)
  const itemsTotal = items.length === 0 ? 0 : basePremiumFor(items[0]);// binding:1, invocation:1 (basePremiumFor), conditional:4, bindings:3 (items.length, items[0]), const:2 (0, 0) = 11
  return itemsTotal + PROCESSING_FEE;                                  // bindings:2, invocation:2 (+) = 4
}
```

Approximate total mass: ~30

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

#### Rule 1: Tests Pass — All 4 tests passing.

#### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
The ternary `items.length === 0 ? 0 : basePremiumFor(items[0])` is a workaround that handles empty arrays specifically. This is a code smell hinting we need to iterate over all items (which we don't yet, per APP — we only have single-item tests). For an empty array, `items[0]` would be `undefined` and `basePremiumFor` would return `undefined`. The conditional is a defensive guard.

Looking at this through APP lens: we're using a **conditional (mass: 4)** to handle empty arrays. Could we eliminate it?

Alternative: Sum the base premiums of all items.
```typescript
const itemsTotal = items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + basePremiumFor(item), 0);
```

But wait — this introduces a **loop (mass: 5)** which is higher than a conditional (mass: 4). Per APP, this would increase mass. However, the next test in the list is "should sum base premiums for two different main items" which will force this change. By APP, we should prefer the conditional over the loop **until forced**.

#### Rule 3: No Duplication — No duplication exists.

#### Rule 4: Fewest Elements — Code is minimal.

### Step 4: Refactoring Decision

Per APP (Absolute Priority Premise):
- Loop (5) > Conditional (4) > Invocation (2) > Binding (1)

We should NOT prematurely introduce a loop. The current conditional+single-item-lookup is the correct minimal solution given current tests. The next test will force us up the APP ladder.

The only minor improvement I could consider: the `_customer` parameter naming uses underscore convention indicating unused — this is intentional and clear.

### Refactoring Evaluation

- Naming: All names clearly reveal intent
- Duplication: None present
- Mass: Already minimal for current test coverage; introducing a loop would increase mass per APP
- Simplification: No unnecessary abstractions; the lookup table is well-suited for the growing type set

**No refactoring performed** — the code is already optimal for the current test set. The recent Green phase only added a single entry to a well-designed lookup table (`staff: 80`), which required no structural change. Introducing a `reduce` loop now would violate APP (loop mass 5 > conditional mass 4) and would be premature given that no current test exercises multi-item summation.

## Refactor Phase Complete

- **Refactoring**: None possible — adding staff to lookup table required no structural changes; lookup table pattern already handles new types cleanly
- **Mass Change**: Unchanged (~30)
- **Tests**: All 4 passing

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Evaluation

**Recent change**: Added rune entry (value 25) to BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE.

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `quote`: Clearly describes purpose - keep
- `basePremiumFor`: Clearly describes purpose - keep
- `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`: Self-descriptive - keep
- `PROCESSING_FEE`: Clear - keep
- `itemsTotal`: Clear - keep

No naming improvements needed.

### Step 2: Current Code Mass

```
BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE map:
- Bindings: 1 (BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE) = 1
- Constants: 10 (5 keys + 5 values: sword/100/amulet/60/staff/80/potion/40/rune/25) = 10

basePremiumFor:
- Bindings: 2 (item, return) = 2
- Invocations: 1 (index lookup) = 2

quote:
- Bindings: 3 (customer, items, itemsTotal) = 3
- Constants: 1 (literal 0) = 1
- Invocations: 2 (basePremiumFor, items.length, +) ~ 4
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary) = 4

PROCESSING_FEE binding: 1 + constant 5 = 2

Total Mass: ~29
```

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 6 tests pass. Verified by recent green phase.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Code is clear and self-documenting. Each constant maps an item type to its base premium. `basePremiumFor` clearly looks up the base for an item.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication. The rune entry simply added another row to the existing map - this is the data-driven design that already eliminated duplication when the second item type was added.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: 
- Could inline `basePremiumFor` since it's a one-liner used once? No - it reveals intent (the name "basePremiumFor" tells you WHY the lookup happens, whereas `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE[items[0].type]` is implementation detail). Rule 2 trumps Rule 4.
- Could remove the ternary by treating empty list uniformly? The conditional handles empty-items case before lookup. Removing would require a different structure (e.g., `items.reduce`), which would have similar mass and currently sums only first item (which is intentional since "two items" test is still todo).

### Step 4: Refactoring Decision

**Refactoring Evaluation**:
- Naming: All names already reveal intent
- Duplication: None present - map-driven design avoids type duplication
- Mass: Adding one data row (rune: 25) adds 2 mass units only, structurally minimal
- Simplification: Cannot reduce without compromising clarity or breaking tests

**No refactoring performed** - the recent change was purely additive (one data entry to an existing lookup map), introducing no duplication, no smells, no clarity issues. The data-driven design already absorbs new item types cleanly. Any structural change would be premature (e.g., extracting an interface or refactoring to handle multi-item until that test arrives).

### Step 5: Report

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: None applied - code already optimal for current test set
- **Reason**: Adding a data row to an existing lookup map introduces no smells; design already DRY and intent-revealing
- **Mass**: Unchanged (~29, +2 from new map entry)
- **Tests**: All 6 passing

Proceeding to the next test.

input.prompt: Impl: src/claim-office.ts. Passing: 7. Added moonstone entry. EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously.

content.text:
**Naming Evaluation**:
- `quote`: Clearly describes function purpose - returns insurance quote
- `basePremiumFor`: Clearly describes helper purpose
- `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`: Clearly describes the lookup table
- `PROCESSING_FEE`: Clear constant name
- All names reveal intent well. No renaming needed.

**Current Code Mass**:
```
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;                          // 1 binding + 1 constant = 2
const BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number> = {
  sword: 100, amulet: 60, staff: 80,
  potion: 40, rune: 25, moonstone: 25,
};                                                  // 1 binding + 6 constants = 7

function basePremiumFor(item) {                    // 1 binding (param) = 1
  return BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE[item.type];          // 2 invocations (lookup, .type) ~ 2 bindings = 4
}

function quote(_customer, items) {                 // 2 bindings = 2
  const itemsTotal = items.length === 0 ?          // 1 binding + 1 conditional + 1 constant + 1 invocation = 7
    0 : basePremiumFor(items[0]);                  // 1 invocation = 2
  return itemsTotal + PROCESSING_FEE;              // 1 invocation (+) + bindings = 2
}
```

Approximate Total Mass: ~26

**Evaluate Simple Design Rules**:

- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 7 tests passing
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Names are clear and self-documenting
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: The recent addition (`moonstone: 25`) duplicates `rune: 25`. However, this is **data duplication that represents distinct concepts** - rune and moonstone happen to have the same base price but are different item types. Extracting a shared constant would couple them artificially and hurt clarity (Rule 2 trumps Rule 3). Looking ahead at the test list, block premiums apply separately to runes and moonstones, confirming they're distinct.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Code is minimal. The `basePremiumFor` helper is a thin wrapper but it does add semantic meaning. With more single-item types and upcoming features (cursed, enchanted, components), keeping the helper is justified.

**Potential Refactorings Considered**:

1. **Inline `basePremiumFor`**: Would reduce mass by ~3 but loses semantic naming. Helper provides intent for upcoming complexity. Reject.

2. **Extract shared price constant for rune/moonstone**: Creates artificial coupling. Reject (Rule 2 > Rule 3).

3. **Simplify `items.length === 0 ? 0 : basePremiumFor(items[0])`**: Could use `items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + basePremiumFor(item), 0)` which generalizes to multiple items (upcoming test). However, this anticipates future tests - violates YAGNI. The current ternary is minimal for current tests. Reject for now.

4. **Type the parameter for `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` lookup more strictly**: Could add `keyof typeof` but adds complexity without functional benefit. Reject.

**Refactoring Evaluation**:

After careful analysis with all four Simple Design Rules:
- Naming: Already clear and reveals intent
- Duplication: Apparent price duplication (25) represents distinct domain concepts (rune vs moonstone); coupling them would hurt clarity
- Mass: Already minimal for current test coverage
- Simplification: No unnecessary abstractions; `basePremiumFor` provides semantic clarity

**Decision**: No refactoring performed.

**Reasoning**: The recent Green-phase change added a single data entry (`moonstone: 25`) to an existing lookup table. This is the ideal way to extend functionality through data rather than code. The implementation remains:
1. Clear in intent (Rule 2)
2. Free of harmful duplication (Rule 3) - the value `25` appears twice but represents two semantically distinct items
3. Minimally complex (Rule 4)

Forcing a refactoring (e.g., extracting `RUNE_AND_MOONSTONE_PRICE = 25`) would create false coupling between independent domain concepts and reduce clarity. Per the principle that "Rule 2 trumps APP," preserving semantic independence is more valuable than eliminating coincidental literal duplication.

**Refactor Phase Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: None applied - documented reasoning above
- **Tests**: All 7 passing
- **Mass**: Unchanged (~26)

Proceeding to the next test.

content.text:
All 14 tests pass.

Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Refactored `quote` to group component items (runes, moonstones) by type. Each component group of exactly 3 gets the block premium (60 G); otherwise items are summed at their base premium. Main items (sword, amulet, etc.) sum their base premiums independently. Processing fee added once at the end.
**Result**: All tests now pass (14 passing, 28 todo)
**Approach**: This is minimal because:
- The grouping is the simplest representation needed to handle multiple component types in one quote
- Block-of-3 detection is preserved as-is (exactly 3 = block, otherwise base premiums) — matching all 5 component-block tests (3 runes, 3 moonstones, 4 runes, 7 runes, 3+3 mixed)
- No premature handling for blocks beyond size 3 — the spec's `2 runes + 1 moonstone` test is still `it.todo`, and 7 runes works correctly via the "not exactly 3 = sum bases" branch
- No optimization, no extra abstractions

input.prompt: Impl: src/claim-office.ts. Passing: 17. Added enchantment surcharge. EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously.

content.text:
All 17 tests still passing.

### Refactored Code Mass

```typescript
const isHighlyEnchanted = (item: Item): boolean =>
  (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD;

const surchargeRate = (item: Item): number =>
  (item.cursed ? CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0) +
  (isHighlyEnchanted(item) ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE : 0);

const mainItemPremium = (item: Item): number => {
  const base = basePremiumFor(item);
  return base + base * surchargeRate(item);
};
```

For `mainItemPremium` itself:
- Bindings: 2 (item, base) = 2
- Invocations: 3 (basePremiumFor, +, *, surchargeRate) ≈ 8
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Assignments: 0 (just const) = 0
- Total: ~10 (was ~46 with everything inline)

The refactor:
- Eliminated `let` and 2 mutating assignments (each mass 6)
- Replaced ifs with ternary expressions in a focused helper
- Named the concept `isHighlyEnchanted` (intent revealed)
- Named the concept `surchargeRate` (intent revealed)

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring**: Extracted `isHighlyEnchanted` and `surchargeRate` helpers; replaced mutation pattern in `mainItemPremium` with declarative composition (`base + base * surchargeRate(item)`).

**Improvements**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Named the "high enchantment" concept and the "total surcharge rate" concept explicitly; `mainItemPremium` reads as a clean formula
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Consolidated the two "if condition then add base*rate" patterns into a single additive surcharge-rate expression
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Eliminated `let` and two `+=` mutations; no more intermediate state

**APP Mass**: `mainItemPremium` reduced from ~46 to ~10 (eliminated 3 assignments and 2 conditionals from this function; small overhead in extracted helpers). Total mass across changed functions decreased.

**Tests**: All 17 passing (25 todo remain).

content.text:
All 21 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Summary

**Refactoring Applied:**

1. **Extracted `loyaltyDiscountRate` helper** (Rule 2 - Reveals Intent)
   - Made the discount logic a named concept, parallel to the existing `surchargeRate` helper
   - Creates symmetry: both customer-level and item-level modifiers are rate functions

2. **Extracted `itemsPremium` helper** (Rule 2 + Rule 4)
   - Encapsulates the multi-step computation of summing main items + component groups
   - The `quote` function now reads as a single clear formula: base premium, apply discount, add fee

3. **Extracted `Customer` type alias** (Rule 2 - Reveals Intent)
   - Eliminates the inline type and gives the concept a name reusable for future loyalty/follow-up logic

**Mass Analysis (quote function only):**

Before:
- Bindings: 8 (customer, items, components, mainItems, mainTotal, componentTotal, basePremium, loyaltyDiscount) = 8
- Invocations: ~9 (filter x2, reduce x2, mainItemPremium, isComponent x2, groupComponentsByType, componentGroupPremium) plus arithmetic
- Conditionals: 1 = 4
- Constants/refs: ~4
- Approximate total: ~35

After (`quote` only):
- Bindings: 3 (customer, items, basePremium) = 3
- Invocations: 3 (itemsPremium, loyaltyDiscountRate, +) plus arithmetic
- Conditionals: 0 = 0
- Approximate total: ~12

The `quote` function mass dropped significantly. Complexity migrated to small focused helpers, each with single responsibility. The arithmetic also simplified: `basePremium - basePremium * rate` became `basePremium * (1 - rate)`, eliminating one binding and one multiplication.

**Tests:** All 21 passing.

Refactor phase complete. Proceeding to the next test.

This means our previous tests "105 G for plain sword", "65 G for amulet" etc. are wrong - they should be 115 G, 71 G (60+6+5), etc. The test list creator interpreted "first insurance" as something optional.

content.text: Now I need to add first-insurance surcharge to the implementation. This will need rounding to be considered too. Let me check current state.

Wait — `claim` is referenced but not implemented. The import succeeds because it's exported. Good. Now I need to:
1. Add first-insurance surcharge (per item)
2. Round final premium UP
3. Make all existing tests pass with new expected values

Let me think about the per-item rounding question. Looking at the values:
- amulet: 60 + 6 = 66 + 5 = 71 ✓
- 1 rune: 25 + 2.5 = 27.5 + 5 = 32.5, rounded UP = 33 ✓
- 2 runes: 50 + 5 = 55 + 5 = 60 ✓
- 7 runes: 175 + 17.5 = 192.5 + 5 = 197.5, rounded UP = 198 ✓
- 3 runes (block): 60 + 6 = 66 + 5 = 71 ✓
- 4 runes: 100 + 10 = 110 + 5 = 115 ✓ 
- cursed sword: 150 + 15 first + 5 = 170? But I wrote 165 G in my updated test... Let me recheck.

Looking at my updated test for cursed sword: "100 + 50 curse + 10 first + 5 fee = 165 G". But is first insurance on the BASE premium (100) or the surcharged premium (150)?

Spec says "A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge." The integration example: "Newcomer with cursed sword (steel, ench 3), 165 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first + 5 fee = 165". So 10 = 10% of base (100), NOT 10% of (base+curse=150). 

Similarly for the follow-up: 100 base + 50 curse + 30 ench - 20 loyalty + 10 first - 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 = 160. So 10 = 10% of base sword (100), not of any total.

So first insurance is 10% of the item's BASE premium (before curse/enchant). Let me re-verify my test values:
- cursed sword: 100 + 50 + 10 + 5 = 165 ✓
- ench 5 sword: 100 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 145 ✓
- cursed + ench 5: 100 + 50 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 195 ✓
- cursed sword + amulet: 100+60 + 50 + 10+6 + 5 = 231 ✓

For loyalty: 20% discount applies to policy base premium (sum of item premiums). Per integration example: 155+5=160 with loyalty. Let me check: items premium = 100+50+30+10 = 190; -20 loyalty -15 follow-up = 155; +5 = 160 ✓. So loyalty applies to (sum of items including curse/ench/first-insurance), then follow-up applies similarly.

Wait, recheck: 100 base + 50 curse + 30 ench - 20 loyalty + 10 first - 15 follow-up = 155. The loyalty (-20) is 20% of what? 20% of 100 = 20. So loyalty is 20% of BASE only? Or 20% of (100+50+30 = 180)? 20% of 180 = 36, not 20. 20% of 100 = 20. So loyalty discount = 20% of policy base premium (sum of item base premiums = 100), NOT of the surcharged total.

Hmm but the spec says "Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a 20 % loyalty discount." And: "policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base premium (the sum of all item base premiums)."

So:
- loyalty: 20% × (sum of item base premiums) → discount
- first insurance: 10% × (sum of item base premiums per item, but each item is "first insurance") → surcharge
- follow-up: 15% × (sum of item base premiums) → discount

For cursed sword: base premium = 100. Curse surcharge = 50% × 100 = 50 (item-specific, added to item base). First insurance = 10% × 100 = 10 (policy-wide). Total = 100 + 50 + 10 + 5 = 165. ✓

For long-standing 2nd quote cursed ench-7 sword: base = 100. Curse = 50. Ench = 30. Loyalty = -20% × 100 = -20. First = 10% × 100 = 10. Follow-up = -15% × 100 = -15. Total = 100+50+30-20+10-15+5 = 160. ✓

So my test values for loyalty: yearsWithMHPCO 2, sword: 100 - 20 + 10 + 5 = 95 ✓.

Now for 2 runes (each rune base 25, sum base = 50):
- first insurance = 10% × 50 = 5
- 50 + 5 fee = 55? But I wrote 60. Let me recalc: items premium (no surcharges) = 50; first insurance = 10% × 50 = 5; total = 50 + 5 + 5 fee = 60. ✓

For 1 rune: 25 + 2.5 first + 5 = 32.5 → 33 ✓.

But wait — for the block tests. The block premium of 60 G for 3 alike components — is this a "base premium"? Yes per spec: "A building block of 3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G."

So for 3 runes (block): policy base premium = 60. First insurance = 10% × 60 = 6. Total = 60 + 6 + 5 = 71. ✓

For 4 runes (no block): policy base premium = 100. First insurance = 10. Total = 110 + 5 = 115. ✓

Good. My values match. Now the structural insight: first insurance is policy-wide based on policy base premium total. That's different from what I told the green agent earlier ("per item"). Let me update the implementation.

Let me dispatch the green agent for the test-list adjustment, since the test list has been updated and the implementation must now handle ALL the new expected values plus rounding. This is more than minimal.

content.text:
Now I need to compute: items premium (per-item with surcharges) + first-insurance (10% of policy base premium = sum of item base premiums, NOT surcharged) - loyalty discount (20% of policy base premium) - follow-up discount (15% of policy base premium) + processing fee, rounded UP.

Wait — what is "policy base premium" for first-insurance? Let me re-verify with the cursed sword integration: items premium with surcharges = 100+50 = 150. First insurance = 10. Loyalty = 0. Follow-up = 0. Total = 150 + 10 + 5 = 165. ✓

For 2nd quote of cursed ench-7 sword: items premium = 100+50+30 = 180. Loyalty = -20. First = +10. Follow-up = -15. Total = 180 - 20 + 10 - 15 + 5 = 160. ✓

So loyalty discount is 20 (= 20% × 100 base). Sum of base premiums = 100 (just one sword's base, not including its surcharges). So policy base premium = sum of item base premiums (without surcharges). First insurance = 10% × policy base premium. Loyalty = 20% × policy base premium. Follow-up = 15% × policy base premium.

But for blocks, the policy base premium for 3 runes = 60 (block) not 75. Let me code it: items premium with surcharges (per-item) + policy base premium adjustments.

Let me define:
- `itemsPremium(items)` = sum of per-item surcharged premiums + sum of component-group base premiums (handles blocks). This is the "policy base premium with item-specific surcharges applied".
- `policyBasePremium(items)` = sum of item base premiums (or block premiums for components). This is what loyalty/first/follow-up are computed against.

Wait — for the test "should sum base premiums with per-item first surcharge (sword+amulet: 100+60+10+6+5 = 181 G)", the calc is per-item: 10 for sword + 6 for amulet = 16 first insurance, or 10% × (100+60) = 16. Same answer.

For 1 rune: 25 base + 2.5 first + 5 = 32.5 → 33. First = 10% × 25 = 2.5.

For 3 runes (block): 60 base + 6 first + 5 = 71. First = 10% × 60 = 6.

For 7 runes (block + 4 singles): 175 base + 17.5 first + 5 = 197.5 → 198. First = 10% × (60+100) = 16. Hmm 175 not 160. Wait, the test description says "175 base + 17.5 first". Let me recompute: 60 (block) + 4 × 25 (singles) = 60 + 100 = 160. But test says 175. 7 × 25 = 175 with no block, OR 60 (block) + 4 × 25 = 160 with one block. Which is correct?

From spec example: "7 runes → 175 G base premium". Hmm, so 175 G is described as the base premium for 7 runes — but my earlier understanding was 60 (block) + 4*25 = 160. Let me re-read spec carefully:

"A building block of 3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G."
"- 7 runes → 175 G base premium"

So spec says 7 runes = 175 G base premium! That means NO block applies when there are 7? Or block only applies to the FIRST 3 and rest are singles giving 60+100=160? But spec says 175.

Wait, let me re-read: "- 2 runes → 50 G base premium, - 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies), - 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3), - 7 runes → 175 G base premium"

OK so 7 runes = 7*25 = 175. "block requires exactly 3". So 4 runes = 100 (no block). 7 runes = 175 (no block, since not exactly 3). Block only triggers if exactly 3 alike components.

So in the implementation, we need to remove the "split a group of N into blocks of 3 + singles" logic — only apply block when the group is exactly 3 items. So 6 runes = 150, not 120. And 3 runes + 3 moonstones = 60 + 60 = 120 (each separate group is exactly 3).

The implementation already does this (`componentGroupPremium` checks `group.length === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE`). 

So 7 runes: 7×25 = 175 base. +17.5 first +5 fee = 197.5 → 198. ✓
6 runes: 6×25 = 150 base. +15 first +5 fee = 170.
4 runes: 4×25 = 100 base. +10 first +5 fee = 115. ✓

So `componentGroupPremium` of a group of 7 runes returns 175 (since not == 3, falls through to sum 7×25=175). Correct.

Now let me update the implementation:

content.text:
Interesting - my compilation error prediction was incorrect. Vitest with the default config does not run TypeScript strict type-checking on test files; it transpiles and runs. So the excess `previousQuotes` property is simply ignored at runtime, and the test fails directly with an assertion error (expected 100, received 115).

Since the test went straight to runtime failure, the two-stage discipline collapses here. Let me document this honestly.

```
Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should apply 15% follow-up discount on customer's second quote (100 + 10 - 15 + 5 = 100 G)"
- Expected: Compilation error from excess property check on Customer
- Actual: No compilation error - Vitest transpiles without type-checking, so the extra `previousQuotes` field was accepted; test went directly to assertion failure.
- Prediction: Incorrect
```

Since the test is now failing at runtime (without needing a separate empty-function step — `quote` already exists), I'll make the runtime prediction matching the actual run.

```
Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should apply 15% follow-up discount on customer's second quote (100 + 10 - 15 + 5 = 100 G)"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 100
- Actual value: 115 (current implementation ignores previousQuotes, so returns 100 + 10 + 5 = 115)
- Diff:
  Expected: 100
  Received: 115
```

This matches the actual output exactly.

Step 7:

```
Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should apply 15% follow-up discount on customer's second quote (100 + 10 - 15 + 5 = 100 G)"
**Compilation Prediction**: "Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'previousQuotes' does not exist in type 'Customer'" Incorrect
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 100, received 115 Correct
**Result**: Test fails with assertion error (expected 100, received 115). The compilation-error stage did not occur because Vitest transpiles without type-checking, so the new `previousQuotes` field is accepted at runtime; the function `quote` already existed, so the test went straight to a runtime assertion failure as predicted in the runtime stage.

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

Notes on the design choice I made for the Green agent:
- API shape chosen: extended the `Customer` object with an optional `previousQuotes?: number` field (count of prior quotes; default 0). The customer is on their second quote when `previousQuotes >= 1`, which triggers the 15% follow-up discount on the policy base premium.
- Files involved:
  - /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts (test activated at line 115)

content.text:
All 25 tests still pass.

**Refactor Phase Complete**

**Naming Evaluation**:
- Reviewed all function names; they reveal intent clearly.
- The two near-duplicate helpers had overlapping concerns, so I unified them under one parameterized helper `sumItemsPremium` which clearly states it sums premiums with a configurable main-item strategy.

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 3: No Duplication)**:
- Extracted shared structure between `itemsPremiumWithSurcharges` and `policyBasePremium` into a single higher-order `sumItemsPremium(items, mainPremium)` function.
- The two functions only differed in how main items were valued (`mainItemPremium` vs `basePremiumFor`); component handling was identical.

**APP Mass Change**:
- Before: two nearly-identical helpers (~12 bindings, 10 invocations, 6 loops, 4 constants for shared structure duplicated). Estimated mass for the two helpers: ~58.
- After: single helper (~5 bindings, 5 invocations, 3 loops, 2 constants). Estimated mass: ~32.
- Net reduction: roughly -26 mass points.
- `quote` itself is unchanged in mass; the two call sites now pass a function reference (+2 bindings vs the prior two function calls equivalent).

**Rule Evaluation Summary**:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 25 tests green.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Improved — single helper makes the symmetry between the two totals explicit; both differ only in their main-item valuation strategy.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): Removed structural duplication of component grouping/reduction.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Reduced from two functions to one.

**Tests**: All 25 passing.

